methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $110M - $200M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Graphyte
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M

private.name: Fourth Power
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $150M

private.name: Steady Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $110M

data.narrative:
Carbyon is a Series A climate and energy technology company founded by Hans De Neve that has raised €22.3M to date. The valuation analysis draws from a peer universe of 9 private companies and 1 public comparable (LNZA), all operating in the climate and energy sector.

The peer-median method forms the basis of valuation analysis. Three same-sector, same-stage peers—Graphyte ($200M), Fourth Power ($150M), and Steady Energy ($110M)—yield a median valuation of $150M, with a range spanning $110M to $200M. This peer cohort represents comparable maturity and capital efficiency profiles within the Climate & Energy space. The broader comparable set includes TAE Technologies and Redwood Materials among private peers, providing additional market context, though the three-peer median carries the most direct methodological weight given stage and sector alignment.

The triangulated valuation range is $128M to $172M, centered on a median of $150M. This relatively narrow range reflects reliance on a single primary method; the analysis would benefit from additional triangulation approaches such as forward-looking revenue multiples or venture capital cost-of-capital analysis, should revenue projections and burn rate data become available. Key uncertainties include technology validation timelines, customer concentration, and capital requirements through subsequent funding rounds. The $22.3M raised to date represents approximately 15% of the median valuation, consistent with typical Series A dilution patterns.
